danger_signals.meaning: successful defensive play anticipation; impending sack or tackle

danger_signals.visual_form: defensive player sprite positioning near receiver
danger_signals.meaning: risk of interception or incomplete pass

danger_signals.visual_form: running back surrounded by defensive sprites with few blockers

safety_signals.visual_form: receiver sprite with clear separation from defenders
safety_signals.meaning: successful passing opportunity; safe play completion likely

safety_signals.visual_form: gap in offensive line with blockers engaged on defenders
safety_signals.meaning: viable running play; ball carrier can advance

safety_signals.visual_form: smooth animation flow after play call
safety_signals.meaning: defensive anticipation was incorrect; offense has advantage

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: offensive formation sprites deploy based on play choice (running vs. passing formation)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: ball carrier or receiver routes execute predetermined path

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: defensive line positioning shifts based on play call selection
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: if correct anticipation: line collapse; if incorrect: defensive gaps appear

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: ball carrier surrounded by defensive sprites with tackle animation trigger
receiver sprite connects with ball sprite; animation shows catch
ball sprite deflects or defender sprite intercepts trajectory
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: possession turns over or downs increment

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: team roster changes display; opponent difficulty increases visually
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: computer-selected opponent team has different playbook effectiveness

environmental_storytelling.example: Miami and San Francisco display three passing plays vs. one running play, signaling aggressive aerial offense strategy vs. other teams' balanced approaches

player names and 1987-1988 season statistics visible in team rosters communicate 'real NFL context' without full league license; revision differences (Eric Dickerson → Albert Bentley) for Indianapolis reflect legal/licensing world-state changes
four plays per team (mostly two running, two passing) communicate strategic depth via limitation; team effectiveness variance suggests player-quality differences
9-10 player count per side (vs. real 11) communicate 'arcade abstraction' while maintaining spatial strategy representation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: cannot advance if surrounded; advances rapidly if lane exists
high interception risk if defender nearby; safe completion if isolated
blocks defenders when anticipation is incorrect; collapses when defense reads play correctly
penetrates backfield and collapses plays on correct reads; gaps exposed on incorrect reads
throw trajectory visible; pass completion/interception determined by receiver/defender proximity

notes: Tecmo Bowl uses minimal visual complexity (NES constraints) to communicate sport state through sprite positioning, animation timing, and play outcome feedback. No explicit danger/safety HUD elements documented; communication is implicit in field layout and player sprite proximity. Faction identity relies on city/state naming (not visual color schemes) due to NFL league license restrictions. The game's manifestation layer is purely mechanical: play success/failure determined by defensive anticipation vs. offensive selection, visualized through sprite behavior.
